reasoning: The wp_remote_get sink in downloadFile() (line 290 of class-favicon-by-realfavicongenerator-api-response.php) does fetch URLs sourced from JSON fields package_url, files_urls[], and preview_picture_url with no host allowlist check inside that function. The code path to reach it is: install_new_favicon() AJAX handler -> build_json_result_url() -> download_result_json() -> new Favicon_By_RealFaviconGenerator_Api_Response($result) -> downloadAndUnpack() -> downloadZipFile()/downloadFilesDirectly()/downloadFile(). Two security controls are present that the finding undercounts: (1) build_json_result_url() enforces the initial JSON fetch URL to https://realfavicongenerator.net (host allowlist via wp_parse_url + strtolower comparison, always prepending the hardcoded HTTPS host), eliminating DNS poisoning and plain MITM as attack vectors since HTTPS certificate validation applies; (2) install_new_favicon() verifies wp_verify_nonce($response->getCustomParameter(), 'favicon_generation') before calling downloadAndUnpack(). The nonce check is not a complete blocker against a compromised API server because the nonce was sent to the API in the original generation request and the compromised server can echo it back. Therefore the actual viable attack path is: full compromise of the realfavicongenerator.net API server (not DNS poisoning, not network MITM). A compromised API can return arbitrary URLs in the JSON body which will be fetched without host validation by downloadFile(). This is a real supply-chain SSRF, but it requires full upstream API server compromise rather than DNS poisoning or MITM as claimed in the finding. The reachability description is overstated. Severity is low rather than medium because the prerequisite is full compromise of a third-party HTTPS service, not an easily exploitable attacker position. Auth requirement is confirmed: nonce-protected AJAX, admin-initiated flow, manage_options capability gating the UI.
